skill.name: exercise-designer
skill.description: Designs evidence-based programming exercises for Python education using retrieval practice, spaced repetition, interleaving, progressive difficulty, test cases, rubrics, hints, and AI-collaborative activities.
skill.summary: Creates structured Python practice sets with learning objectives, difficulty progression, tests, rubrics, and evidence-based review.
skill.author: 92Bilal26

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows the alerts are false positives from Markdown examples, educational terminology, templates, and fenced command snippets. The only confirmed operational concern is a local Python helper script that reads a user-provided YAML file path with yaml.safe_load; this is a low-risk local filesystem/script capability, not malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: Local YAML Helper Script Reads User-Supplied Path
low_findings.description: The helper script accepts a command-line exercise file path, checks whether it exists, reads it, and parses YAML with safe_load. This is legitimate local tooling for exercise review, but users should only run it on trusted local exercise files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file-read behavior is explicit and uses a user-provided path, but parsing uses yaml.safe_load and there is no network, shell execution, or secret access. The risk is limited to expected local helper behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Alerts Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external-command findings are Markdown fenced examples and instructions such as reading reference files or running the included helper script. They do not create hidden shell execution, command interpolation, or autonomous command execution inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Reviewed locations are educational Markdown examples or explicit user-facing usage instructions. No evidence found of command injection, obfuscated execution, or unapproved shell behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Terminology False Positives
low_findings.description: The high and low blocker alerts appear to match educational words and template fields, not cryptographic implementations or reconnaissance logic. Reviewed files contain exercise design guidance, YAML templates, and rubric text rather than malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is pedagogy and exercise authoring, and the executable script does not use cryptography or reconnaissance commands. No evidence found of weak crypto use, host probing, process inspection, or data collection.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local File Read in Helper Script
dangerous_patterns.description: The included helper script reads the path supplied on the command line. This should remain documented as local-only tooling and should not be used on untrusted files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is real local file access, but it is expected for a command-line helper and uses safe YAML parsing. It warrants caution but not a publication block.

content.value_statement: Programming educators need practice sets that build retention, not only problem volume. This skill creates structured Python exercises with objectives, progression, tests, rubrics, hints, and review patterns.
actual_capabilities: Designs varied Python exercise sets using fill-in, debug, trace, refactor, build-from-scratch, and AI-collaborative formats.
Maps exercises to learning objectives, success evaluations, difficulty bands, and Bloom taxonomy levels.
Applies retrieval practice, spaced repetition, interleaving, elaboration, concrete examples, and desirable difficulty.
Adds progressive hints, test case expectations, answer keys, and assessment rubrics for each exercise.
Reviews existing exercise sets for variety, progression, strategy coverage, test coverage, and learner fit.
Provides a local helper script that suggests missing normal, edge, and error test cases from an exercise YAML file.
limitations: The guidance is optimized for Python programming education, not all academic subjects.
It depends on clear learning objectives or chapter specifications for best results.
The helper script suggests test coverage heuristically and does not execute learner code.
Research references are summarized for design guidance and are not a substitute for curriculum validation.

use_cases.title: Build a New Practice Set
use_cases.description: Create a short sequence of exercises for a Python topic with objectives, difficulty progression, hints, tests, and rubrics.

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Homework
use_cases.description: Review a problem set for repeated exercise types, missing edge cases, weak scaffolding, or limited spaced repetition.

use_cases.title: Create AI-Collaborative Activities
use_cases.description: Design exercises where learners use AI as a student, co-worker, teacher, or output that must be validated.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Beginner Exercise Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with one topic and clear audience.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create 4 beginner Python exercises for loops. Include learning objectives, exercise type, difficulty, estimated time, hints, tests, and a short rubric.

prompt_templates.title: Add Spaced Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request that mixes current and prior concepts.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design 5 dictionary exercises for Lesson 4 that review lists from Lesson 2 and conditionals from Lesson 3. Show which concepts each exercise practices.

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate an Existing Set
prompt_templates.scenario: Quality review for a draft assignment.
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate these 8 exercises for variety, difficulty progression, evidence-based strategies, test coverage, and rubric quality. Recommend specific improvements.

prompt_templates.title: Design From Success Evals
prompt_templates.prompt: Using these success evaluations and learning objectives, create an evals-first exercise sequence with starter, core, and stretch tasks that prepare learners for mastery.

output_examples.input: Create exercises for beginner Python list methods.
output: A 5-exercise set with fill-in, trace, debug, build, and explain tasks.
Each exercise includes objectives, difficulty, time estimate, hints, tests, and rubric criteria.
The sequence starts with scaffolded syntax practice and ends with independent application.

output_examples.input: Review these loop exercises for learning quality.
output: A diagnostic review of exercise variety, difficulty jumps, missing prior-concept review, and weak edge cases.
Actionable revisions, such as adding trace-execution and debug-this tasks before open-ended coding.
A revised progression that balances current content with spaced repetition.

output_examples.input: Create an AI-collaborative exercise for validating generated code.
output: A task where learners inspect AI-generated code, identify risks, write tests, propose fixes, and document a validation checklist.
Assessment criteria for vulnerability detection, test coverage, fix quality, and reflection.

best_practices: Start with specific learning objectives and success evaluations before writing exercises.
Mix exercise types and include normal, edge, and error cases for programming tasks.
Use spaced repetition by combining the current concept with recent and older concepts.
anti_patterns: Do not create many identical build-from-scratch exercises without scaffolding or review.
Do not skip rubrics, hints, or test cases when learners need clear success criteria.
Do not treat AI-generated code as correct without requiring learner validation and reflection.

faq.question: What kind of exercises can this skill create?
faq.answer: It can create fill-in, debug, trace, explain, refactor, test-driven, build-from-scratch, and AI-collaborative programming exercises.

faq.question: Does it only support Python?
faq.answer: The references and templates focus on Python, but many design principles can be adapted to other programming languages.

faq.question: Can it grade student submissions?
faq.answer: No. It designs rubrics and test expectations, but it does not run or grade student code.

faq.question: How does it use learning science?
faq.answer: It applies retrieval practice, spacing, interleaving, elaboration, concrete examples, and desirable difficulty to exercise design.

faq.question: Can it help with existing assignments?
faq.answer: Yes. It can review an exercise set for variety, progression, objective alignment, test coverage, and assessment clarity.

faq.question: Is the included script required?
faq.answer: No. The script is optional local tooling for suggesting test case coverage from an exercise YAML file.
